text: Robotic ventral hernia.
One of the greatest advancements with the use of robotics in general surgery has been the development of ventral hernia repair and abdominal wall reconstruction.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to perform extensive adhesiolysis, employing flexible scissors and dissection hooks to carefully separate adhesions within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to close primary hernia defects, demonstrating the precision and control afforded by robotic instruments.
The surgeon uses wristed robotic instruments to enhance the functional architecture of the abdominal wall, preparing to suture the mesh precisely to the abdominal fascia.
The result is a hernia patient with a much more functional repair, a patient with lower rates of hernia recurrence, and significantly less wound complications.
In addition, there's markedly less pain postoperatively.
The surgical field reveals two hernia defects, with the larger defect located closer to the umbilicus.
It's typical of an incisional hernia that has developed following a previous operation.
The surgeon clears the peritoneum and preperitoneal fat to expose the posterior rectus fascia, preparing a clean surface for mesh placement against the abdominal wall.
The surgeon has cleared enough surface area for mesh placement and is now focusing on closing the hernia defect.
The monopolar hook is an excellent tool for this process.
The surgeon passes a suture through one of the laparoscopic ports to initiate the closure of the hernia defect.
The surgeon uses a zero-sized V-lock barb suture to close the defect in the abdominal wall, following the preparation of the area for mesh placement.
The surgeon uses a V-lock barb suture with one-way barbs to close the defect, ensuring tension is retained with each pass of the suture.
The surgeon makes relaxing incisions laterally in the posterior rectus fascia to reduce tension during the closure of the defect.
The surgeon carefully positions mesh over the relaxing incisions made in the posterior rectus fascia, ensuring complete coverage to support the closure.
The surgeon closes the defect in the abdominal wall, enhancing its integrity as part of the hernia repair, while preparing for mesh reinforcement.
The result is a strong repair and a lower recurrence rate for the patient.
The surgeon introduces the mesh through an eight millimeter port after completely closing the defect, preparing to reinforce the abdominal wall.
I prefer the Ethicon PhysioMesh because of its ease of handling and the wide pore construction.
A needle is attached to the center of the mesh and introduced through the fascial closure, emerging through the abdominal wall to secure the mesh in place for suturing.
The surgeon sutures the mesh to the abdominal wall, securing it in place as part of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses endo-wrist instrumentation to perform suturing movements, mimicking the techniques of open surgery, as the mesh is secured to the abdominal wall.
The surgeon places circumferential sutures around the mesh, ensuring secure fascial purchase with each needle bite, as the mesh is positioned against the abdominal wall.
This replaces the need for trans-fascial suturing and surgical tack placement that is necessary during laparoscopic repair.
This also makes robotic hernia repair less expensive on a case by case basis when compared to laparoscopic repairs due to the need for less disposable instrumentation.
It is more efficient, more effective, and absolutely less painful for the patient.
In our own practice series,

Out of two hundred and seventy repairs, we have had three recurrences, which is a one point one recurrence rate over the past three years, and a three point three rate of seroma formation, none of which have been clinically significant.
In addition, there have been no serious wound infections.
Thus far in our experience, a very effective and safe approach to hernia repair.
In addition,
Since it is almost identical to suturing during an open procedure, the learning curve for new robotic surgeons is virtually non-existent.
Operative times have been comparable with laparoscopic operative times as well as open procedures.
One of the myths perpetuated about robotic surgery is that longer operating times and longer setup times are a necessary evil.

However, our center has debunked both of those myths and shown that a team approach with a dedication to efficiency can produce equal times in the operating room as well as turnover time between cases.
The surgeon completes the hernia repair, with the surgical site visibly closed and secured.

The surgeon completes a robust primary closure of the hernia defect, ensuring symmetric tension-free placement of the mesh without overlap. A circumferential suture line is applied to secure the mesh to the fascia.
Really a typical result.
